profiles.name: fable
profiles.description: PICK for the stages where a wrong answer is costly: judge panels, final synthesis, high-stakes code review, planning and architecture, subtle correctness work (concurrency, tricky invariants), long autonomous implementation, and frontend work needing real design taste. It is the premium worker ($10/$50 per 1M, ~2x sol) with deliberate multi-minute turns, so it shines on digests and decisions rather than raw fan-out — let luna/terra gather, then hand fable the synthesis. Give it big, well-framed asks and it delivers polished, dependable results in one pass. For security-focused analysis, pair it with or defer to sol, the specialist there. Capped at 3 concurrent / 6 calls per run — budget it for the verify/judge/synthesize stages.

profiles.name: luna
profiles.description: PICK for the wide cheap tier: fan-out sweeps, scouting, exploration, inventory/grep-style tasks, first-pass bug finding, mechanical broad changes, and high-volume classification — the cheapest and fastest worker ($1/$6 per 1M) with real capability for routine coding. Ideal as the gathering layer of any workflow: sweep wide with luna, then route the digest to a stronger model for depth. Keep its tasks scoped and self-contained; for long multi-file grinds, deep architectural reasoning, or visual polish, hand off to terra or sol, and double-check fast-moving factual claims against a source.

profiles.name: sol
profiles.description: PICK for complex mixed work at mid cost ($5/$30 per 1M): implementation grinds, difficult debugging, migrations, repo-wide changes, root-cause analysis — and a first-rate adversarial reviewer and verification panelist, including security-focused review where it specializes (and where fable defers). SOTA-tier agentic coding with unusually token-efficient output, and no concurrency cap, so it scales beautifully for parallel review panels and multi-voter verification. It works boldly and thoroughly: on legacy code, pin the scope explicitly, and skim the diff after long autonomous runs — its ambition occasionally outruns the brief. Route trivial edits and wide fan-out to luna/terra to save budget for what sol does best.

profiles.name: sol-max
profiles.description: PICK as the closer for the hardest bounded coding work: open-ended complex bug fixes, deep debugging in large unfamiliar repos, long implementation grinds with architectural constraints, and high-stakes adversarial review or red-team-style verification where sheer persistence wins — the same gpt-5.6-sol as 'sol', run at max effort, so it digs deeper and simply does not give up. Give it clear constraints and a definition of done and it will grind until it gets there; a quick diff-and-test check afterward is a worthwhile habit on big autonomous refactors. Reserve it for tasks that genuinely need the extra depth — terra/luna cover routine work at a fraction of the cost. Capped at 4 concurrent / 8 calls per run.

profiles.name: terra
profiles.description: DEFAULT generalist and the right first choice for most standard work: multi-file implementation, bug diagnosis with verified fixes, code review, refactoring plans, and technical research — reliable, production-oriented, and cheap ($2.50/$15 per 1M) with no concurrency cap, so it also scales for parallel mid-weight work. Thorough by nature: it verifies before it answers, which is exactly what you want on real changes (nudge it toward brevity for quick lookups). Step down to luna for high-volume trivial edits, and escalate the very hardest reasoning, verification, or taste-critical frontend work to sol-max or fable. When routing is uncertain and stakes are moderate, pick terra.
